iti76:1.2: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti76:1.3:
"Aspiring to these three forms of bliss, a wise person should guard his virtue. Which three?
'May praise come to me,' a wise person should guard his virtue.
'May wealth come to me,' a wise person should guard his virtue.
'At the break-up of the body, after death, may I reappear in a good destination, in heaven,' a wise person should guard his virtue. Aspiring to these three forms of bliss, a wise person should guard his virtue."
Intelligent, you should guard your virtue, aspiring to three forms of bliss: praise; the obtaining of wealth; and, after death, rejoicing in heaven.
iti76:1.4: Even if you do no evil but seek out one who does, you're suspected of evil. Your bad reputation grows.
iti76:1.5: The sort of person you make a friend, the sort you seek out, that's the sort you yourself become — for your living together is of that sort. The one associated with, the one who associates,
iti76:1.6: The one who's touched, the one who touches another — like an arrow smeared with poison — contaminates the quiver. So, fearing contamination, the enlightened should not be comrades with evil people.
iti76:1.7: A man who wraps rotting fish in a blade of kusa grass makes the grass smelly: so it is if you seek out fools.
iti76:1.8: But a man who wraps powdered incense in the leaf of a tree makes the leaf fragrant: so it is if you seek out the enlightened.
iti76:1.9: So, knowing your own outcome as like the leaf-wrapper's, you shouldn't seek out those who aren't good. The wise would associate with those who are. Those who aren't good lead you to hell. The good help you reach a good destination.
iti76:1.10: This, too, was the meaning of what was said by the Blessed One, so I have heard.
